does: This paper investigates whether Popperian falsification prompts improve code generation in large language models by testing the actual contribution of the Popperian reasoning content versus generic scaffolding effects. The authors conduct a pre-registered two-tier ablation study comparing a standard Popperian skill against three controls: a length-matched placebo prompt, a headers-only scaffold stripped of procedural content, and an execution oracle using HumanEval+ unit tests. They also employ a vocabulary-halo sentinel and same-model self-judging audit to control for LLM-as-a-judge biases. Testing on Claude Sonnet 3.5 (N=163) and Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B, they find that on the frontier model all conditions cluster near ceiling performance without significant separation, failing to support the reported +5-point improvement, while results on the smaller model show marginal gains only from the execution oracle.
connects: This work responds to the rapidly expanding use of reasoning prompts and 'skills' in LLM-based code generation, building on practices like chain-of-thought prompting and scientist-inspired reasoning frameworks. It directly challenges prior claims about Popperian falsification improving code quality, which were established through LLM-as-a-judge evaluation—an approach with known issues including positional bias, self-preference bias, and stylistic preferences documented in recent LLM evaluation literature. The paper extends methodological critiques of LLM evaluation by applying pre-registered, controlled ablation designs to separate confounds in prompt engineering, and builds on earlier work questioning whether apparent improvements from complex prompts reflect genuine capability gains or artifacts of evaluation procedures.
enables: This work establishes a methodological template for rigorously evaluating code-generation prompts and reasoning skills, showing how execution oracles and careful ablations can ground claims beyond LLM-as-a-judge verdicts. It points toward future research investigating which scaffolding elements genuinely enhance code generation versus which are mere evaluation artifacts, and suggests the need for more systematic study of prompt engineering effects on smaller, open-source models where ceiling effects may not obscure true differences. The paper enables practitioners to view popular reasoning frameworks with appropriate skepticism and motivates development of more robust, execution-based evaluation methods for code generation that can detect real improvements even when models approach performance ceilings.
